1: "When the LORD your God brings you into the land that you are entering to take possession of it, and clears away many nations before you, the Hittites, the Girgashites, the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites, seven nations more numerous and mightier than you" — the seven nations: this list appears in various configurations throughout the OT (Exodus 3:8,17; Deuteronomy 23:23; Deuteronomy 33:2; Deuteronomy 20:17; Joshua 3:10; Deuteronomy 9:1; Deuteronomy 12:8; Deuteronomy 24:11). The number seven may represent completeness (all Canaanite peoples) rather than a precise historical roster. Archaeological identification of these groups is partial: Hittites in Canaan are likely Anatolian migrants or a Canaanite group using the name; Jebusites held Jerusalem (2 Samuel 5:6-9); Amorites are attested in Mesopotamian sources as Semitic peoples of the Syrian-Palestinian region. The Girgashites and Perizzites are not attested outside the OT.
2: "And when the LORD your God gives them over to you, and you defeat them, then you must devote them to complete destruction (herem). You shall make no covenant with them and show no mercy to them" — the herem (devoted destruction / ban) in comparative ANE context: the Mesha Stele (ca. 835 BCE, Moab) is the closest ANE parallel, where King Mesha describes devoting the Israelite city of Nebo to Chemosh by killing all its inhabitants. The cherem ideology appears in several ANE conquest inscriptions: defeating enemies was dedicated to one's god by total destruction. In Israel, the cherem was YHWH's claim on conquered territory, preventing commercial exploitation of the victory and preventing cultural contamination through treaty-alliance with the defeated nations.
3: "You shall not intermarry with them, giving your daughters to their sons or taking their daughters for your sons" — the intermarriage prohibition: the ground (v4) is specifically religious, not ethnic. The concern is that intermarriage leads to adopting the spouse's deities. Solomon's intermarriages (1 Kings 11:1-8) are the paradigmatic fulfillment of this warning: his foreign wives "turned his heart after other gods." The Book of Ruth, by contrast, shows a Moabite woman absorbed into Israel through covenant loyalty rather than a Moabite man taking an Israelite woman into his religious orbit. The prohibition is directional: the danger is the Canaanite religious influence entering Israel, not the other way around.
4: "For they would turn away your sons from following me, to serve other gods. Then the anger of the LORD would be kindled against you, and he would destroy you quickly" — the stated rationale for the intermarriage prohibition: apostasy risk. The religious-contamination logic is consistent throughout the OT (and is invoked by Ezra-Nehemiah in the post-exilic period, Ezra 9-10). The historical validation: every major apostasy episode in Israel's history (golden calf, Baal-Peor, Baal of Tyre under Ahab, etc.) has an intermarriage or foreign-deity-adoption element.
5: "But thus shall you deal with them: you shall break down their altars and dash in pieces their pillars and chop down their Asherim and burn their carved images with fire" — the four destruction targets: (1) altars (mizbeachot), (2) pillars (massevot, standing stones), (3) Asherim (wooden poles representing the goddess Asherah), (4) carved images (pesilim). Archaeological excavations of Canaanite cult sites (like Megiddo, Hazor, and Lachish) have found all four types of cultic objects. The Bronze Age Canaanite cult was richly physical: outdoor high places, incised stone pillars, wooden goddess symbols, and divine figurines. Deuteronomy 7:5 is a systematic checklist for dismantling this entire material religion.
6: "For you are a people holy to the LORD your God (am qadosh lYHWH). The LORD your God has chosen you to be a people for his treasured possession (am segulah), out of all the peoples who are on the face of the earth" — the election theology: am qadosh (holy people) and am segulah (treasured possession/peculiar treasure). Segulah (H5459) appears in Ugaritic and Akkadian contexts as a king's personal property treasure, something distinct from the general treasury — a private collection of special value. Israel as YHWH's segulah is the covenant community as the divine king's personal treasure. 1 Peter 2:9 applies this to the church: "a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession."
7: "It was not because you were more in number than any other people that the LORD set his love on you and chose you, for you were the fewest of all peoples" — the anti-merit election: YHWH's election of Israel is explicitly not based on numerical greatness (rav). The ground of election is entirely from YHWH's side (v8: "because the LORD loves you and is keeping the oath that he swore to your fathers"). This is the OT's clearest statement of unconditional election: Israel did not earn the relationship, and its continuance is not secured by Israel's performance but by YHWH's faithfulness to the oath he swore to Abraham. Paul in Romans 9:10-16 draws the same conclusion from the election of Jacob over Esau before birth.
8: "But it is because the LORD loves you and is keeping the oath that he swore to your fathers, that the LORD has brought you out with a mighty hand and redeemed you from the house of slavery, from the hand of Pharaoh king of Egypt" — the twin grounds of election: YHWH's love (ahavah) and his oath (shevuah) to the patriarchs. The love is not explained further — it is bedrock: YHWH loves because he loves (v8 does not explain why he loves). The oath introduces covenant continuity: the Exodus is the keeping of the promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Genesis 12:1-3; Deuteronomy 15:18; Deuteronomy 22:15-18). Election is both gracious (love without merit) and historical (oath to the fathers).
9: "Know therefore that the LORD your God is God, the faithful God (el hane'eman) who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments, to a thousand generations" — el hane'eman (the faithful/reliable God): the word ne'eman (H539) is from the same root as amen and emet (truth). YHWH's covenant faithfulness extends to a thousand generations of those who love him. The covenantal statistics: curse reaches three-four generations (Deuteronomy 5:9); faithful love extends to a thousand. The ratio (ca. Deuteronomy 250:1) makes the covenantal economy overwhelmingly weighted toward mercy. This is the character of the God who keeps covenant — his faithfulness exceeds his judgment by orders of magnitude.
10: "And repays to their face those who hate him, by destroying them. He will not be slack with one who hates him. He will repay him to his face" — the reciprocal covenant consequence for those who hate YHWH: the covenant's unconditional election does not guarantee unconditional protection. Election is the framework of relationship; faithfulness or unfaithfulness within that relationship determines blessing or curse. The phrase "to his face" (el panav) emphasizes personal accountability — no proxy judgment, no delayed accounting, but direct personal consequence.
11: "You shall therefore be careful to do the commandment and the statutes and the rules that I command you today" — the conclusion of the election theology section: the response to YHWH's faithful election is careful covenant keeping. The word shamor (be careful/keep) closes the theological arc that began with the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) and election (Deuteronomy 7:6-10): knowing the character of YHWH and the ground of election produces not presumptuous security but motivated obedience.
12: "And because you listen to these rules and keep and do them, the LORD your God will keep with you the covenant and the steadfast love that he swore to your fathers" — the treaty-blessing condition: covenant faithfulness from Israel triggers YHWH's covenant faithfulness to them. The structure is not a merit-system (Israel does not earn YHWH's love by obedience) but a covenant-system (Israel's faithfulness is the covenant's expected mode of operation). The blessings follow as covenant consequence, not moral reward. Deuteronomy 28's blessing-curse list (the sanction section of the treaty form) is the extended development of this principle.
13: "He will love you, bless you, and multiply you. He will also bless the fruit of your womb and the fruit of your ground, your grain and your wine and your oil, the increase of your herds and the young of your flock, in the land that he swore to your fathers to give you" — the comprehensive material blessing: the covenant blessing covers the full agricultural economy (grain, wine, oil) and pastoral economy (cattle, sheep). In the ANE world, these material blessings were attributed to Baal (storm god bringing rain for grain), Asherah (fertility), and El (cattle). The Deuteronomic covenant claims all these blessings as YHWH's gift — the covenant monotheism means YHWH, not Baal, controls the rain; YHWH, not Asherah, controls fertility. The Baal-cycle mythology that explained rain and agriculture is replaced by covenant theology.
14: "You shall be blessed above all peoples. There shall not be male or female barren among you or among your livestock" — the infertility-reversal blessing: barrenness was the most feared personal disaster in the ancient world, where children were the primary economic and social security system. The blessing of full fertility for humans and livestock covers both the personal (family continuity) and economic (herd maintenance) dimensions. Rachel's prayer (Genesis 30:1: "Give me children or I shall die") captures the cultural weight that makes this covenant blessing so powerful.
15: "And the LORD will take away from you all sickness, and none of the evil diseases of Egypt that you knew will he put on you, but he will lay them on all who hate you" — the health-covenant: the Egyptian diseases may refer to the plagues (in reversal: no more plagues for Israel) or to the endemic diseases of the Nile Delta (schistosomiasis, malaria, dysentery). In the ANE world, health and sickness were attributed to divine favor and disfavor. YHWH's covenant with Israel includes health protection — a stark contrast to the disease-producing deities of Egypt (who were placated through elaborate ritual). The complete health-blessing is realized eschatologically in the new creation (Revelation 21:4: "no more death or mourning or crying or pain").
16: "And you shall consume all the peoples that the LORD your God will give over to you. Your eye shall not pity them, neither shall you serve their gods, for that would be a snare to you" — the no-pity command: lo tachos einecha (your eye shall not pity). The covenant's military program is maintained by suppressing the natural human response of mercy toward conquered peoples. The snare-warning (moquesh) explains the no-pity command: any preserved Canaanite population and its religious infrastructure will be a trap for Israel's covenant faithfulness. The historical validation: wherever Canaanite populations were preserved (the Gibeonites, Josh 9; the Canaanites of Judges 1-2), apostasy followed.
17: "If you say in your heart, 'These nations are greater than I. How can I dispossess them?'" — the heart-question as the key battlefield: the military challenge of dispossessing numerically/militarily superior nations will produce the same heart-question the spy-generation asked in Numbers 13. Deuteronomy anticipates the temptation and addresses it before it arises in the land. The catechetical format ("if you say in your heart... then remember") is educational pre-emptive strike: address the doubt before the situation produces it.
18: "You shall not be afraid of them but you shall remember what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and to all Egypt" — the Pharaoh-paradigm as anti-fear medicine: what YHWH did to the greatest imperial power of the ancient world (Egypt under Ramesses II or Thutmose III) is the basis for confidence against the Canaanite city-states. The Amarna Letters (ca. 1350 BCE) show the Canaanite city-states writing to the Egyptian pharaoh for military help against various threats — Egypt was the overlord of Canaan during this period. Israel is told not to fear powers that were themselves client-states of the power YHWH had just defeated.
19: "The great trials that your eyes saw, the signs, the wonders, the mighty hand, and the outstretched arm, by which the LORD your God brought you out. So will the LORD your God do to all the peoples of whom you are afraid" — the Exodus-as-template: what YHWH did in Egypt (signs, wonders, mighty hand, outstretched arm) he will replicate against the Canaanite nations. The stereotyped Exodus description ("mighty hand and outstretched arm") appears 20+ times in Deuteronomy. By making the Exodus the template for all future divine action, Deuteronomy establishes the Exodus as the paradigm event of covenant history — the lens through which all subsequent YHWH-action is to be understood.
20: "Moreover, the LORD your God will send hornets among them, until those who are left and hide themselves from you are destroyed" — the hornet weapon: tzir'ah (H6880, hornet/wasp) is mentioned in Exodus 23:28 and Joshua 24:12 as a divine weapon. Whether literal (plague of hornets) or metaphorical (panic, or perhaps the Egyptian hornet as the symbol of Pharaoh — Pharaoh was depicted with the hornet in Egyptian royal iconography) is debated. Either way, it represents a hidden divine weapon beyond Israel's military resources that pursues those who hide from Israel's army.
21: "You shall not be in dread of them, for the LORD your God is in your midst, a great and awesome God" — the divine-presence-as-courage: YHWH eloheicha beqirbecha el gadol venora. The same divine presence that makes the camp holy (Deuteronomy 23:14) makes Israel's position in battle unassailable in principle. The Ark of the Covenant (the physical symbol of YHWH's presence) traveled with the Israelite army precisely because the army's power was derivative of divine presence. Uzzah's death (2 Samuel 6:6-7) and the Philistines' capture of the Ark (1 Sam 4-5) are narratives that explore the dangerous power of YHWH's presence.
22: "The LORD your God will clear away these nations before you little by little. You may not make an end of them at once, lest the wild beasts grow too numerous for you" — the gradual-possession strategy: meat meat (little by little). The practical reason given (wild animal population control) reflects the agricultural reality of the ancient Levant: depopulated land quickly becomes wilderness that large predators (lions, bears) inhabit. The gradual-displacement strategy also reflects YHWH's mercy toward the Canaanites — giving more time before the judgment falls (cf. Genesis 15:16: "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete").
23: "But the LORD your God will give them over to you and throw them into great confusion, until they are destroyed" — the divine-panic weapon: hamam (H2000, to confuse/throw into panic). YHWH's military strategy includes supernatural terror sent into the enemy's hearts (cf. Joshua 2:9: Rahab reports that Israel's God sent terror before them; Joshua 10:10: YHWH threw the enemies into panic at Gibeon). The concept of the panic-inducing divine warrior appears throughout ANE military texts but is given its distinctive covenant-theological shape in Deuteronomy and Joshua.
24: "And he will give their kings into your hand, and you shall make their name perish from under heaven. No one shall be able to stand against you until you have destroyed them" — the royal-destruction command: capturing and destroying the enemy king is the decisive act of ancient conquest. The Canaanite city-state structure was king-centered (as the Amarna Letters show): destroy the king, and the city's political structure collapses. The book of Joshua records the defeat of thirty-one kings (Joshua 12:24), fulfilling this promise systematically.
25: "The carved images of their gods you shall burn with fire. You shall not covet the silver or the gold that is on them or take it for yourselves, lest you be ensnared by it, for it is an abomination to the LORD your God" — the idol-wealth prohibition: the silver and gold decorating the idols are toevah (abomination) because they are integral to the cultic system. Achan's sin (Josh 7) involves taking devoted items — the cherem is extended to divine-image wealth. The temptation of the image's material value (silver and gold) is precisely the snare Deuteronomy 7:25 warns against: the economic logic of "I'll take the silver but not worship the idol" doesn't work because the material is inseparable from its cultic meaning.
26: "And you shall not bring an abominable thing into your house and become devoted to destruction like it. You shall utterly detest and abhor it, for it is devoted to destruction" — the contagion principle: cultic objects bring their dedicated status (cherem) into any space they enter. The idol in the house makes the house a cherem-object. The double-abhor formula (shaqatz teshaqetzenu vetaev teta'avenu) — the intensified construction of total rejection — is the strongest possible language for complete avoidance. The household as covenant space (the mezuzah marks the threshold; the doorpost-written law marks the walls) cannot simultaneously harbor idols. YHWH's presence and the idol's presence are incompatible.
